response:
to solve this problem we must first store the value of the factorial in our mental memory.
***
we need to store the count of prime factors within one hundred factorial to proceed.
***
the number of factors of five in one hundred factorial is twenty, which we must store.
***
the number of factors of two in one hundred factorial is ninety six, so we store that too.
***
when we divide by ten to the power of fifty we remove fifty pairs of two and five.
***
after this division we store the remaining count of factors of five which is zero.
***
the remaining factors of two are forty six after we subtract fifty from ninety six.
***
since the numerator still has factors of two the fraction can be simplified further.
***
we store the fact that the denominator becomes one because all fives are gone.
***
the final answer is one because we store the result of this irreducible fraction.
